title: AI and Robots Are Coming for Every Job — White Collar First

source.original: You Have 10 Years to Join the AI Economy | Michael Saylor

body_html:
The automation wave is arriving in two distinct phases. White-collar AI bots are already here, capable of drafting legal documents, providing medical opinions, composing sophisticated contracts, and performing analytical work that previously required years of expensive education. The cost of a complex estate plan that once ran $10 million will soon collapse to $10 — and the quality may be indistinguishable from what a team of elite lawyers would produce.

The second wave — physical robotics — is roughly a decade away. When a billion robots can walk, build, paint, install, and deliver, the cost of blue-collar labor will fall exponentially, just as the cost of white-collar expertise is falling now. Self-driving vehicles are an early indicator: when the car drives itself, the entire logistics and transportation economy restructures around near-zero marginal labor cost.

This twin disruption systematically demonetizes human capital. A law degree, a medical credential, twenty years of professional expertise — all of these are becoming less valuable as AI replicates them at scale. The analogy is precise: just as tractors made raw physical strength economically irrelevant, AI makes raw intellectual effort economically irrelevant. No matter how talented or hardworking you are, you cannot outwork a machine that operates 24/7 at near-zero cost.

The winners in this new economy will not be the most skilled or the hardest working. They will be those who own distribution, influence, networks, and platforms. An account with 5 million followers can monetize AI-generated content instantly. A company like Amazon or Apple, with last-mile distribution to hundreds of millions of households, can capitalize on every new wave of automation. The imperative is clear: stake your claim in assets, platforms, and networks now — before the transformation is complete.

For anyone starting a career, the advice is stark: do something nobody has ever done before, using tools that didn't exist a generation ago. Spending £200,000 on a law degree may be a worse investment than spending the same amount on scarce digital property. The value of being a lawyer — or any knowledge worker defined by the ability to draft, analyze, or advise — is on a downward trajectory that will only accelerate.
